actions.thought: Q5s is a marginal hand even 3-handed from the button. Without any reads on the opponents, the safest and standard play early in the tournament is to fold and wait for better spots to commit chips.

I am in the small blind position with Jack of Hearts and Trey of Hearts. Since the button has folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. J3 suited is a marginal hand, but it is acceptable to play in a heads-up small blind versus big blind scenario to see a flop relatively cheaply. I will complete the big blind by calling to see the action unfold.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c4c. The Button folded and the Small Blind completed. Since I have a speculative suited hand and have position on the Small Blind for the rest of the hand, checking to see a free flop is the most plus-EV play here. Raising would likely not generate enough folds to justify the risk with such a marginal hand.

I am holding Jack-high on an Ace-high, Queen-high board. I have no pair, no draw, and very little equity against a Big Blind's calling range, which is heavily weighted toward Aces and Queens. Betting here would be an unproductive bluff. Checking is the most standard and conservative play to navigate this hand without committing unnecessary chips.

The flop of Qs 2c As is very unfavorable for my hand (7c 4c). I have no pair and no direct draw. Flashlight checked from the Small Blind, indicating they might have a weak hand or are checking to me. Since the pot is small and I have zero showdown value, I will check behind to see a free turn card. Betting here is risky because this board hits many ranges, and I don't want to invest chips with such a weak holding.

On the turn, the 3 of spades gives me a pair of 3s, but this is a very weak hand given the board texture of Ace-high and Queen-high. My pair is likely beaten by any Ace, Queen, or even a higher pair of 2s or 3s with a better kicker that my opponent might hold. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands, and checking keeps the pot small, allowing me to potentially see a cheap river and fold if any aggression comes from my opponent.

The turn card is the 3s, which completes a potential spade flush. My hand, 7c 4c, has no value and very little equity. Flashlight has checked twice, which could indicate weakness, but since I have zero showdown value and the pot is small, there is little incentive to bluff on a board that contains an Ace, a Queen, and three spades. I will check behind to see a free river card and hope to either improve to a straight or simply get to showdown cheaply if the board checks down.

The board shows Qs-2c-As-3s-2d. I hold Jh-3h, giving me a pair of 3s. Given the board texture contains an Ace, a Queen, and a paired 2, my pair of 3s has extremely low showdown value and is beaten by any Ace, Queen, or pocket pair. Betting here has no value as it would only be called by superior hands, and there is no need to bluff when checking is free. Checking is the standard play to reach showdown as cheaply as possible.

I have 7-high on a board of Qs-2c-As-3s-2d. The Small Blind has checked every street. While I have no showdown value, the pot is very small (200) and the board texture (Ace, Queen, three spades) makes it difficult to execute a convincing bluff after checking the whole way. I will check behind and concede the pot if I am beaten, which is highly likely.

4d 2h is an extremely weak hand with no real playability or showdown value. There is no incentive to call or raise with this holding, so folding is the correct strategic decision to preserve my stack.

I have been dealt pocket Kings, the second strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Since the Button has folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. I need to raise for value and to protect my equity. A standard raise from the Small Blind in this situation is typically between 3x and 4x the big blind. I will raise to 350 to build the pot and take control of the hand.

We are in the Big Blind with J6s. The Small Blind has opened to a relatively large size of 3.5x. While we would have positional advantage post-flop, J6s is a weak holding with limited playability and is easily dominated by the Small Blind's raising range. It's best to fold and preserve our stack early in the tournament.

Ace-Six offsuit is a standard opening hand from the button in a three-handed game. I will raise to 2.5 times the big blind to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot with positional advantage.
Holding 83o in the Small Blind facing a Button raise is a clear fold. The hand is extremely weak, disconnected, and unsuited, making it unplayable, especially out of position.

Queen-Three offsuit is a very weak hand, even in the Big Blind against a Button open. Although the pot odds are somewhat attractive, this hand has poor realization equity and is frequently dominated by the opponent's opening range. Since this is early in a 150-hand tournament, I prefer to conserve my stack and fold rather than playing a marginal hand out of position.
Holding 93o on the button pre-flop. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em and offers very little playability or equity. Easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind and the Button has folded. I hold King-Nine offsuit, which is a strong enough hand to play aggressively in a heads-up scenario against the Big Blind. Raising allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop. A raise to 300 is a standard size to apply pressure while maintaining a reasonable stack-to-pot ratio.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 4s, a very weak starting hand. Flashlight raised to 300 from the Small Blind after the Button folded. Even though I am getting 2:1 odds on a call, 64o is near the bottom of my range and performs poorly post-flop. There is no reason to defend such a weak hand out of position or even in a blind-vs-blind situation against a standard 3x raise early in the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

K-Q offsuit is a strong hand in position (Button). As the first to act pre-flop, I should open the pot to build value and potentially isolate the blinds. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that balances aggression with stack conservation.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 300. In a three-handed game, Button opening ranges are typically quite wide. While A4o is not a premium hand, it has decent high-card value and some playability. Calling allows me to see a flop for 250 more, although I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Given the stack depths and the early stage of the tournament, I will call to see if I can connect with the flop, while being mindful of the Big Blind still left to act behind me.

With Jack-Nine offsuit in the Big Blind facing a 3bb open from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, our hand is easily dominated and plays poorly out of position in a multiway pot. Although we are getting decent pot odds to call, playing marginal offsuit connectors out of position against two players is a losing proposition over the long run.

On this flop of 7h-8c-3h, I have missed completely with my Ace-Four offsuit. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to them and see how they react. I have some backdoor possibilities and Ace-high might still be ahead of some of the Button's range, but there is no reason to lead out and bloat the pot with a weak hand.

The flop of 7-8-3 is low and disconnected, which likely missed both ranges. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have the initiative. A continuation bet of 350 into the 700 pot should be sufficient to represent a strong hand and take down the pot immediately, given my opponent checked to me and is unlikely to have connected strongly with this board.

I have missed the flop entirely with Ace-high on a board that favors many parts of the Button's opening range. Flashlight has made a continuation bet of 350 into a 700 pot. Given that I have no draws and am out of position, I have very little equity to continue in this hand. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play here.

I am on the button with QJo, which is a very strong hand in three-handed play. Standard strategy is to open-raise to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds. A sizing of 2.5x to 3x the big blind is standard. I will raise to 250.

87 offsuit is too weak to defend from the small blind against a button raise. We will be out of position for the rest of the hand with a marginal, unsuited connector. Folding is the correct and standard play here.

Holding 2-5 offsuit is a very weak hand with no playability post-flop. Even though I have decent pot odds to call the 150 chips, it is not worth entering a pot out of position with such a dominated range. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

With A7o on the button, I have a decent ace and the best position at the table. Raising to 2.5x (250) is a standard open to either steal the blinds or play heads up in position with a range advantage.

Holding Ace-Queen suited is a very strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. Since the Button opened, I should 3-bet to take the initiative and maximize value, rather than calling and playing out of position against the Button and potentially the Big Blind. A raise to 850 is an appropriate sizing to either take down the pot pre-flop or build a pot with a significant equity advantage.

I am holding J3o in the Big Blind. The Button opened to 250, and the Small Blind 3-bet to 850. J3o is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially against a 3-bet range. Given the action, I have no reason to continue and should fold to preserve my stack.

I opened A7o from the button as a standard steal, but faced a strong 3-bet from the Small Blind. A7o plays very poorly against a 3-betting range, as it is heavily dominated by stronger Aces (A8-AK) and performs badly against pocket pairs. Continuing with this hand would be a mistake. The best play is to fold and preserve my stack.

Queen-three offsuit is a weak starting hand with poor connectivity and kicker strength, giving it minimal equity against the ranges of my two opponents. Even with the positional advantage of the button, there is no strategic value in playing this hand. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision.

I am in the Small Blind with King of Clubs and Three of Spades. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Pro). While K3o is a weak hand, in a blind-vs-blind situation, it is strong enough to attempt a steal. Pro has shown a willingness to fold to pre-flop aggression in earlier hands. I will raise to 300 to try and take down the pot immediately.
Facing a 3x raise from the small blind, my 4h5s is too weak and off-suit to defend profitably from the big blind. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am on the button with QTo, which is a strong opening hand in 3-handed play. I want to take control of the pot and use my positional advantage. A standard raise to 3x the big blind is appropriate here to put pressure on the blinds.

Facing a 3x raise from the button, Q3 suited is too weak to call or 3-bet from the small blind. Being out of position for the rest of the hand and with the big blind still left to act, folding is the most fundamentally sound decision.

The opponent on the button opened to 300. As the big blind, I hold Ace-Four offsuit, which is a standard defensive hand given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650). The opponent has shown frequent aggressive opening behavior in previous hands, so calling is a solid play to defend my blind and see a flop with a reasonable hand.

I am currently out of position as the Big Blind against the Button. I have the nut flush draw with the Ace of Diamonds on a board containing two diamonds. Checking is the standard play here to allow the aggressor to potentially c-bet, which will give me the option to either call to realize my equity or raise if I choose to semi-bluff. Leading out from the Big Blind is generally not optimal here as it bloats the pot out of position without a made hand.

I have top pair (Tens) with a Queen kicker on a board that contains both flush and straight draws (Td 6d 7s). Flashlight checked to me, the pre-flop aggressor. I should make a continuation bet to extract value from weaker hands and to protect my hand against the various draws available on this board. A bet of roughly 60% of the pot is appropriate here.

I hold the nut flush draw with the Ace of Diamonds on a board featuring two diamonds (Td, 6d). With a pot size of 1000 and a bet of 400, I am getting sufficient pot odds to continue. Given I am out of position against an aggressive opponent, calling is the most balanced and calculated play to realize my equity. Raising as a semi-bluff is possible, but against an active opponent, calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to reassess on the turn if I hit my flush, an Ace, or pick up additional equity.

The turn card (5s) improves my hand to an open-ended straight draw (needing an 8 or 3) to complement my nut flush draw. I currently hold significant equity against most of the button's range. Given that I am out of position, checking is the most prudent and balanced strategy. This allows me to gauge the opponent's aggression and betting size before deciding whether to call to realize my equity or potentially check-raise to maximize pressure if the situation allows.

The turn 5s is a potentially dangerous card as it completes a straight for 89 and adds more straight draws. Flashlight called my flop bet, indicating they have a piece of the board or a strong draw. With Top Pair/Queen kicker, I have a solid hand but it is not invulnerable. Checking back here controls the pot size and avoids a potential check-raise on this wet board. I will look to see a river and evaluate based on the final card.

I hold Ace-high with Ace-Four offsuit. The board is Ten, Six, Seven, Five, King (with only four diamonds present, so I do not have a flush). My hand is only Ace-high, and I am out of position. The button checked the turn, which may indicate a weak hand or a desire for pot control. Given the board texture and the strength of the button's pre-flop range, betting as a bluff is unlikely to be profitable, as they are likely to call with any pair. Checking is the most prudent action to reach a cheap showdown.

I have second pair with a Queen kicker. The King on the river is an overcard, but Flashlight's check-call on the flop followed by two checks suggests they may have a marginal hand or a missed draw. With decent showdown value, checking back is the most prudent play to win the pot without risking a check-raise.

I am on the Button with Q9o. This is a weak, offsuit hand that does not play well post-flop, especially when facing potential 3-bets or calls from the blinds. In a 3-handed game, I should be opening slightly wider, but Q9o is still too marginal to risk 200 chips. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.

With 5h-Th in the small blind and the button having folded, I have a decent opportunity to play a hand in position relative to the BB's action, though I will be out of position post-flop. The hand is a suited connector, which plays well enough to complete the small blind and see a cheap flop against the big blind. This is a low-risk way to realize equity.

Flashlight limped from the small blind after Pro folded. Holding A5o in the big blind, I have a solid hand against a typical limping range. I will check to see a free flop and maintain pot control, as raising might bloat the pot out of position with a hand that is often the best but vulnerable to a reraise.

The flop is Qs-7d-9d. My hand (5h-Th) has no pair and no significant draw. Betting here as a bluff would be ill-advised against the big blind who could easily have a Queen or a piece of the board. Checking is the most prudent approach to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

Flashlight limped from the small blind and has now checked the flop. My Ace-high has some showdown value, but the board (Qs 7d 9d) is relatively coordinated and likely hit a fair portion of a limping range. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and get to see the turn for free, which is beneficial since I have no immediate draw or made hand better than Ace-high.

I have improved to a pair of 5s on the turn. My opponent (Flash) has shown weakness by checking the flop. However, a pair of 5s with a 10 kicker is a marginal hand that functions best as a bluff-catcher rather than a value-betting hand. I should check to keep the pot small and reach a cheap showdown, avoiding unnecessary risk against a potentially wider or capped range.

I have improved to a pair of fives on the turn. Flashlight limped from the small blind and has checked both the flop and the turn, indicating a weak range or a passive approach. While my bottom pair might be the best hand, the board is fairly coordinated with straight and flush draws. By checking back, I control the pot size and ensure I reach the river with a hand that has showdown value, without risking a check-raise from a stronger marginal hand like a 7 or a 9.

The board is Qs-7d-9d-5c-Kd. My hand is 5h-Th, giving me a pair of 5s. The action has checked through every street. My pair of 5s has very little showdown value, but at this point, checking is the only logical move. Betting would turn a marginal hand into a bluff, which is unlikely to succeed given that my opponent could hold a King, Queen, or even smaller pairs or straight draws that might have paired up. Checking allows me to reach showdown for free to see if my pair of 5s is sufficient, which is the standard play in this spot.

Flashlight has checked every street after limping pre-flop, which indicates a very weak range or perhaps a missed draw. My pair of fives has some showdown value in this small pot. There is no merit in betting here, as better hands will not fold and worse hands are unlikely to call. I will check back and hope my pair is good for the showdown.

T9 offsuit is a standard hand to open from the button. Stealing the blinds is a key part of maintaining stack pressure and taking initiative in late position, especially against two opponents. A raise to 250 is a common standard open size in this format.

In a 3-handed game, Ace-Seven offsuit is a relatively strong hand against a standard Button opening range. While 3-betting is a viable aggressive option to take the pot immediately, calling from the Small Blind allows me to see a flop and realize my hand's equity without bloating the pot out of position. I will call the additional 200 chips to see the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with a weak, unsuited hand (Q5o). The Button raised and the Small Blind called. Although I am getting good pot odds, playing a hand like Q5 offsuit out of position against two active players is a losing proposition. Folding is the best decision.

I have Ace-high on a Jack-high board. As the Small Blind who called a pre-flop raise, my standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading into the pre-flop raiser here doesn't accomplish much as I have limited equity against their continuing range, and I want to see how they react to the board before committing more chips.

The flop is 4s Jh 3h. Although my Ten-Nine doesn't have a strong connection to this board, I hold the betting initiative from my pre-flop open. This is a relatively dry texture where a continuation bet is standard to represent strength, such as a Jack or a pocket pair, and potentially force a fold from hands like 5-5 or 6-6. Checking here would surrender initiative and make it difficult to realize any equity. A bet of 300 (half the pot) is a balanced sizing to maintain pressure.

On a board of 4s-Jh-3h, my Ace-high has very little equity against Flashlight's continuation betting range, especially being out of position. Without a flush draw or a pair, and only one overcard to the Jack, calling a 300-chip bet into a 900-chip pot is not profitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with 5c 2h, which is one of the weakest hands in Hold'em. While I have positional advantage, this hand lacks any real potential for improvement and is not strong enough to justify a raise or even a call. I'll wait for a better opportunity.

With King-Ten offsuit in the small blind and the button folding, I have a strong enough hand to open with a raise to put pressure on the big blind. Raising takes the initiative and forces the big blind to defend with a wider range or fold.

My hand is 3-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in No-Limit Hold'em. Despite the pot odds offered, playing such a weak hand out of position against an opening raise from the small blind is statistically unfavorable. Folding is the correct exploitative and mathematical decision to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the Button with 2c 5d offsuit, which is one of the worst possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. It has no high card value, suitedness, or meaningful straight potential. The only mathematically sound and optimal play here is to fold and wait for better opportunities.

With 5-3 offsuit in the small blind, the hand is too weak to justify a call or a raise. While I have already invested 50 chips, there is no strategic value in defending the blind with such a poor holding. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play to preserve my stack.

Being on the button in a three-handed game allows for wide opening ranges. 5c7c is a suited connector that plays well in position, making a raise a strategic move to potentially steal the blinds or take initiative into the flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Trey offsuit (Js 3d), which is a very weak hand. Flashlight has raised from the Button to 300. While ranges are wider in 3-handed play, J3o does not play well out of position and is not a strong enough candidate for a 3-bet bluff or a call. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.
6d 9h is a weak, uncoordinated, and offsuit hand. Facing a 3x open from the Button, it is not profitable to defend from the Big Blind. The clear decision is to fold and wait for a better spot.

In a 3-handed game, the button should open with a wide range of hands to apply pressure on the blinds. 89o is a reasonable hand to open for a raise from the button as it has decent connectivity and I will have positional advantage for the rest of the hand if called. A standard 3x raise to 300 is used to stay consistent with my previous opening sizes.

T5o is a weak, disconnected offsuit hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Button while sitting in the Small Blind, playing out of position with this holding will be highly unprofitable. Folding is the standard and best decision here.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Defending against a button raise with such a hand is negative expected value, regardless of the pot odds. It is best to fold and conserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

Holding 98 offsuit on the button is generally too weak to open-raise profitably. Even though I have position, offsuit connectors like this lack the playability and equity retention needed when called or 3-bet by the blinds. Given that the opponents have shown they can be aggressive and defend their blinds, folding is the best play to conserve my stack for a more profitable spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 5-7 offsuit. This is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position post-flop. Since the Button has folded, I am facing the Big Blind. Given the weak nature of my hand and the disadvantage of being out of position, the most disciplined play to maximize my stack is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

My hand, King-Three offsuit, is extremely weak. In a three-handed game, even though I am on the button, this hand lacks the strength to profitably play against two opponents. Folding is the most disciplined and prudent strategy to preserve my stack at this stage of the tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with 63o, which is a very weak hand. After the button folds, I only have the Big Blind to contend with. However, 63o is significantly below the threshold for a profitable complete or raise from the Small Blind, especially since I will be out of position for the rest of the hand if the Big Blind calls. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Q3 offsuit. While it is a 3-handed game, Q3o is significantly weak to open-raise with, and calling is not a standard play from the button. Given the tournament status and the frequency of aggression from the blinds in previous hands, I will choose the conservative route and fold this hand to wait for a better starting hand.

With 3s7h offsuit in the small blind, my hand has very little equity against almost any two cards the big blind might hold. Given the lack of high-card strength, suitedness, or connectivity, the mathematically optimal play is to fold rather than completing the blind or raising.
Holding 7-3 offsuit on the Button in a 3-handed game is an extremely weak starting hand. There's no strategic reason to play this hand, so the optimal move is to fold.

With Ace-Queen offsuit in the small blind and the button having folded, I have a clear advantage over the big blind's range. This is a premium hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure and attempt to take down the pot pre-flop or define the hand strength early.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. Flashlight raised to 300 from the Small Blind. While I would have position post-flop, J6o is a weak hand that does not play well against a 3x open. Given the tournament context and my current lead in chips, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand and risk being dominated or forced to fold on most flops.

I am on the button with King-Six suited. This is a decent hand to open the pot from the button to steal the blinds. My opponents have been relatively active, but I have position on them. A raise to 300 is a standard open size to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot immediately.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 5d. Flashlight (BTN) has opened for 300. While suited, 8-5 is quite weak to call a 3x raise out of position, especially with Pro still to act in the Big Blind. My pot odds are roughly 35%, but the playability of this hand out of position is poor. I have been playing aggressively when entering pots myself, and calling here would be a passive play with a sub-optimal hand. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision in this spot.

Facing a 3x raise from the Button, Q3s is too weak to call or 3-bet out of position. It has poor playability post-flop, will often be dominated by better Queens, and its flush potential isn't strong enough to justify a call here. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am on the button with Ah 5h, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. My standard strategy from the button is to open with a raise to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. I will raise to 300 chips, which is consistent with my previous sizing in this tournament.
I am dealt 5s3d in the Small Blind. This is a very weak offsuit hand. Facing a raise to 300 from the Button, the correct play is to fold and save my chips for a better opportunity.

Flash has been opening frequently from the button, indicating a wide range. My hand, 10c 9s, is a standard defense in the big blind against a button open. With 200 to call to win a pot of 450, I am getting 2.25:1 odds, making this a mathematically sound call to see a flop given the implied potential of the cards.

The flop is dry and hits the pre-flop raiser's range better than mine. As the out-of-position player, leading out with a weak hand like Ten-high is generally suboptimal. Checking allows me to control the pot size, potentially induce a check-back from Flash, or re-evaluate on the turn if Flash bets.

I have middle pair (5s) with an Ace kicker on a King-high board. Since Flashlight checked to me, I will place a continuation bet to take control of the hand and protect against potential draws (like the flush draw). A bet of 400 (about 60% of the pot) is a standard sizing here.

My hand, Ten-high, has no pair and only weak backdoor possibilities on a King-high board. Flash's continuation bet indicates at least some piece of this board. With no significant equity, calling here would be a mistake, as I would be forced to fold on most turns or continue to bleed chips. Folding is the most disciplined approach to preserve my stack.
3c 9c is a very weak suited hand. Being on the button, it is not strong enough to open-raise or call, so the correct action is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Jack-five offsuit is a weak hand with no real coordination or high card strength. Playing out of position against the Big Blind with such a poor hand is generally unprofitable in the long run. Folding is the most disciplined and effective strategic choice here.

With 4d 8h, I have a very weak starting hand with no significant equity, even in a 3-handed game. As the button, playing such a hand is mathematically suboptimal as it offers no real post-flop playability. Preserving my stack for stronger cards is the priority, so folding is the correct strategic decision.

I have A9o in the small blind after the button folded. This is a strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially end the hand right here, rather than limping and allowing the big blind to see a free flop.

Flash is raising from the Small Blind when folded to, which usually indicates a wide steal range. With A4o in the Big Blind, we are slightly ahead of a wide range, but the hand plays poorly post-flop and can be easily dominated by stronger Aces. However, we have the positional advantage post-flop and the pot odds are attractive (calling 200 to win a pot of 600). Defending by calling is a standard and relatively low-variance play.

I am out of position on a board that coordinates well with the Big Blind's defending range (Js-Ts-3c). My Ace-high has some showdown value but lacks a direct draw. I will check to see how the opponent reacts, as a continuation bet into this wet texture is risky when I have completely missed.

Flash checked on a wet Js Ts 3c board. With just Ace-high and no draws, checking back for pot control and a free card is the most prudent play. We still have some showdown value, and betting opens us up to being check-raised by a stronger range.

I have Ace-high on a board of Js-Ts-3c-6d. After my pre-flop raise was called by the Big Blind and we both checked the flop, the 6d on the turn doesn't change much. Since the opponent checked back on a wet flop, their range is likely capped, but Ace-high still has some showdown value. I will check again to keep the pot small and see if I can get to a cheap showdown or evaluate based on a river bet.

Flash has checked twice on a wet board, indicating weakness or a missed draw. My Ace-high still has decent showdown value against missed broadways or weaker draws. I have no strong draws to semi-bluff with, so checking again for pot control and aiming to see a cheap showdown is the safest play. Betting might only get called by better hands like pairs or better Aces.

The board has paired Jacks on the river (Js Ts 3c 6d Jh) after both players checked the flop and turn. My Ace-high (Ah 9d) has some showdown value against missed draws and weaker hands in the Big Blind's range. Since the opponent has shown no aggression, betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I may already have the best hand. I will check to reach a cheap showdown.

On the river, the opponent checks for the third time. We have Ace-high which might still be good against missed flush draws or missed straight draws like KQ/Q9. Turning our Ace-high into a bluff doesn't make much sense since the opponent will likely fold worse hands and only call with better ones like a stubborn pair. Checking back ensures we get to a free showdown and realize our equity.

I am on the button with 5c 3c. While suited connectors and gappers have some value in three-handed play, 53s is at the very bottom of an opening range. Given the active play of Flashlight and the solid stack of Pro, I will choose to fold this hand to maintain a tight-aggressive image and wait for stronger holdings to continue my momentum.

I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak and unconnected offsuit hand (8-5 offsuit). The Button folded. Playing this from out of position against the Big Blind is a long-term losing play. The optimal decision is to simply fold and preserve my stack for better starting hands.

On the button with A7o, this is a standard open. The hand has strong high-card value, blockers to premium aces, and we will have position post-flop if called. A raise to 2.5x the big blind is an optimal sizing to apply pressure to the blinds.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game. With suited cards, Jd-7d is a reasonable hand to use as a blind steal attempt to try and pick up the blinds without a post-flop showdown, especially given that my position is the most advantageous.

Q9o is a marginal hand to play out of position from the Small Blind against a Button open. The board is Kh-Ts-Ac-7d. I have Jh-9s, giving me a gutshot straight draw to the broadway straight. The river has been dealt and the board is Kh-Ts-Ac-7d-2d. I hold Jh-9s, which is currently just Jack-high with no pair or better. I have a pair of Tens with a Jack kicker on a board of A-K-T-7-2. My opponent in the Big Blind has checked all three streets after calling my pre-flop raise. 98o is a weak offsuit connector. In a 3-handed game, acting first from the button, we should be stealing with a reasonable range, but 98o falls just slightly below the threshold for a standard open-raise. Given the deep stacks, folding to preserve chips for a better spot is the most solid play.

With Queen-Five offsuit in the Small Blind and the Button having folded, I have a profitable opportunity to see a flop against the Big Blind for a small additional cost. Completing the blind is the standard play here to realize equity in position post-flop.

I flopped bottom set (3s 3c) on a fairly dry, disconnected board (3-8-J rainbow). As the pre-flop caller out of position, checking to the pre-flop raiser is the most standard and profitable play. I am on the button with Ace-King high on a dry board of J-8-3. My opponent checked to me, and as the pre-flop raiser, this is a standard continuation bet spot. I flopped bottom set on a relatively dry board. While check-calling is an option to keep bluffs in, we are out of position with deep stacks (around 90 BB effective). Check-raising targets top-pair hands (AJ, KJ, QJ), overpairs (QQ-AA), and straight draws (T9, Q9, 97) that will call, setting up a very favorable stack-to-pot ratio for the turn and river.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 5h, an extremely weak offsuit hand. Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, calling here out of position would be unprofitable. A fold is the only correct decision.

I hold top pair with an Ace, but my kicker (Nine) is weak and the board has a flush draw potential. Checking behind in position is the standard, prudent play to control the pot size. I hold trip Aces (Ad on an Ace-high board with two Aces). The opponent's lead on the turn could indicate a King, a weaker Ace, or a bluff given the board is relatively dry. I hold trip Aces with a Nine kicker. Flash led the turn and checked the river, which suggests a weak King or a missed draw. On the river, the board is Ac-2h-Kc-Ah-7c. I have Trip Aces with a King-7 kicker (from the board). My best hand is A-A-A-K-7. I lose to any Ace with a kicker of 8 or higher (A8, A9, AT, AJ, AQ, AK), any flush (since the 7c completed the club draw), and any full house. Given the pot odds of 5.5:1, I only need to be right (or split) a small fraction of the time to make this call profitable. Folding Trip Aces in this spot would be too exploitable, and raising would only get called by hands that beat me.

On a paired board of 2c-Th-2h, Flashlight has led for a small sizing of 200 into a 600 pot. I have Queen-high with two overcards to the board. Given the excellent pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 1000, requiring 20% equity) and the fact that I have position on the Small Blind for the rest of the hand, a call is the most standard play. My hand has sufficient equity against a wide Small Blind opening and continuation-betting range, and I can potentially take the pot away on later streets if the opponent checks, or win if I improve to a pair of Queens or Nines.

I am holding Ace-high on a paired board. My opponent called a small flop bet, which suggests they have at least a piece of the board (a pair or a draw) or are floating. Betting again into a calling range here is likely to get called by better hands, and checking allows me to control the pot size. I will check to see how my opponent reacts and re-evaluate on the river.

On the turn, the 3 of clubs gives me a flush draw to go along with my two overcards (Queen and Nine). Flashlight has checked after leading on the flop, which often indicates a marginal hand or a surrender. While I could bet to try and take the pot down now, checking back is a very strong option in position. It allows me to take a free card to realize my equity and potentially hit my flush or a pair on the river without the risk of being check-raised. Given the board is paired (Deuces), it's possible Flashlight is pot-controlling with a Ten. I will check behind and see the river.

I am at the river with Ace-high on a board of 2c-Th-2h-3c-9s. My opponent called my flop bet, which suggests they likely hold at least a pair of 10s or 9s, or a mid-to-low pocket pair. Betting here would be a bluff against a range that is very likely to call. Checking is the standard play to minimize losses and reach showdown.

On the river, I have improved to a pair of Nines with a Queen kicker on a board of 2c-Th-2h-3c-9s. Flashlight has checked for the second street in a row after leading on the flop, which strongly suggests a weak to medium-strength hand or a missed draw. While I have showdown value, a value bet is risky because it's unlikely a worse hand (like Ace-high or a small pocket pair) calls, while a better hand (like a Ten or a slow-played Deuce) certainly will. By checking back, I guarantee a showdown and most likely win the pot against Flashlight's current range which consists mostly of missed draws and weak pairs.

8-10 offsuit is a weak, uncoordinated starting hand that performs poorly in multi-way pots. Folding is the disciplined choice, preserving my stack of 9700 for a better opportunity to steal the blinds or play a stronger starting hand.

In a blind-on-blind situation with KJo, I have a significantly stronger hand than the average Big Blind range. Raising to 300 (3x the BB) is standard to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the Big Blind, or simply win the blinds and antes immediately.

Facing a 3x open from the Small Blind, Q5 suited is a marginal hand. Even though we will have position post-flop, the 5 kicker is very weak, and it can often lead to dominated scenarios if we pair our Queen. Given our stack size and the fact that we don't want to play bloated pots with weak holdings, folding is the most solid and low-variance decision here.

I am on the button with 5c 4c, a suited connector that plays well in multi-way pots but is also a good candidate for a speculative open raise to steal the blinds. Given my healthy stack size of 11,500 chips (115 big blinds), I can afford to open this hand and look to play post-flop with position and initiative. My standard opening raise size has consistently been 300 throughout the tournament, and I will maintain that sizing here.

I have a pocket pair of Sevens in the Small Blind facing a Button open. 77 is too strong to fold and plays better as a 3-bet to isolate the wide Button opening range, while discouraging the Big Blind from squeezing or overcalling. I will 3-bet to 900 to take the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c-Tc. Flash on the button opened, and Pro in the Small Blind 3-bet to 900. My hand is a suited connector, but it is dominated by most of the hands that would be raising from the Small Blind in this spot. Playing a 3-bet pot out of position against two opponents with this equity is not advisable. The cost to call is 800, and with my stack size, it is better to preserve my chips for a more favorable spot.

I opened from the button with 5c 4c, a speculative hand that relies on realizeable equity and implied odds. Pro has 3-bet me to 900 from the Small Blind. While I have position, 5c 4c is near the bottom of my opening range and does not stand up well against a typical 3-betting range from a competent player like Pro. Calling 600 more (nearly 10% of Pro's remaining stack) with such a weak hand is likely -EV, as I will often be forced to fold on the flop or find myself dominated. Given my chip lead and the stage of the tournament, I will make the disciplined fold and look for a better spot.

I am on the Button with King-Queen offsuit (KQo), which is a very strong starting hand in this position. Since the action is folded to me or I am first to act, I want to come in with a standard raise to apply pressure on the blinds, build the pot, and take the betting lead. A raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) is an optimal sizing to accomplish this.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (4-6 offsuit). Even though the price to call is relatively small compared to the stack, playing this hand out of position against a button raise is negative expected value. Folding is the standard and correct strategic play.

I have Ace-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind against a button raise from Pro. This is a strong hand in 3-handed play. While a 3-bet is possible, calling the 150 additional chips is a solid way to proceed, keeping Pro's wide button range in the pot and utilizing my chip lead to see a flop without inflating the pot unnecessarily out of position.

I have flopped top pair with a ten kicker on an Ace-high board. In this scenario, checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) is the standard and most effective play. It allows Pro to continuation bet with his wide button opening range, including bluffs and weaker hands that might fold if I lead out. My hand is strong, but not invulnerable, and checking keeps the pot manageable while I evaluate Pro's reaction to this texture.

As the pre-flop aggressor on an Ace-high board, I have a strong range advantage since my raising range includes many Ax hands. A small continuation bet of roughly 1/3 pot (200 chips) puts pressure on the Big Blind's underpairs and missed broadway cards, allowing me to win the pot right here relatively cheaply. If I get called, I can easily reassess on the turn.

I have flopped top pair with a Ten kicker on an Ace-high board, which is a very strong hand in three-handed play. Pro's bet of 200 into a 550 pot (approximately 36% pot) is a standard continuation bet that could represent anything from a strong Ace to a total bluff or a semi-bluff with a draw. Raising here would likely force out his bluffs and weaker hands while only getting called by hands that have me beat or are flipping. By calling, I keep Pro's wide range in play, allow him to continue bluffing on the turn, and keep the pot at a manageable size while I evaluate the next card.

I have top pair with a Ten kicker on an Ace-high board. While this is a strong hand in 3-handed play, it is a classic mid-strength hand that benefits from pot control. By checking, I allow Pro to continue betting with a wide range, including bluffs, semi-bluffs like spade draws or gutshots, and weaker Aces. Leading into the pre-flop aggressor here often narrows his range to hands that either beat me or have significant equity, while checking keeps his wider, weaker range in play and provides me with more information based on his bet sizing and frequency.

On the turn, the 8 of clubs doesn't change the board texture much. Since Flash called my c-bet on an Ace-high flop, his range is heavily weighted towards Ax hands, underpairs, or draws. I have King-Queen high with no equity to improve to a strong draw, making a second barrel unlikely to succeed against his calling range. Checking behind controls the pot size and allows me to see a free river card.

On the river, the board is 4s-As-5h-8c-9h. I have top pair with a Ten kicker (Ac Ts). Pro's check on the turn after his flop continuation bet suggests a medium-strength hand like a weaker Ace (A2-A7) or a pocket pair, as he would likely have bet again with a very strong hand (AJ+) or a set to protect against draws. The 9h on the river is mostly a blank, although it does complete a potential straight for 67 (unlikely) and improves A9 to two pair. By checking, I allow Pro to bet his bluffs or check back his medium-value hands, ensuring I reach showdown with a hand that is very likely the winner in this three-handed dynamic without risking a raise from the small portion of his range that could have improved.

On the river, the board completes with a 9h and Flash checks again. Since I checked the turn, my range is capped and looks like a weak Ax, a pocket pair, or a given-up draw. My King-Queen high has very little showdown value, but it does beat busted flush and straight draws. Bluffing here is risky because Flash's range still contains many weak pairs and Ax hands that called the flop and might stubbornly call a river bet. The most prudent play is to check behind, minimize further losses, and occasionally win against a missed draw.

Qc 5d is a weak starting hand with poor playability, especially offsuit. Since I am the first to act on the button and the cards do not justify an opening raise, folding is the most disciplined decision to preserve my stack.

I have pocket fives on a board of 6c-7c-Kh. This is a poor flop for my hand as both the 6 and 7 are overcards to my pair, and the King is firmly in the pre-flop raiser's range. I hold a pocket pair of sixes on the button, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I want to take initiative and potentially win the blinds or build a pot, so raising is the optimal play over just calling.
